revision.text: '''Suwa Shinto shrine''' was constructed in 1624, after the [[Tokugawa]] government had taken power and begun forcing Christians to reconvert to Buddhism. In 1634 a great autumn festival was held at the shrine. This marked the beginning of the famous [[Kunchi]] festival which continues to this day. Kunchi festival is held on October 7 to 9 every year and is considered one of the three major festivals of Japan, being designated as an Important Intagible Cultural Asset. Every year, thousands of Nagasaki citizens visit Suwa shinto shrine to pray for a year of peace and prosperity. Passing under the huge entrance torii arch, and climbing the first flight of stone steps, the visitor notices a deity devoted to piurification on the left. "Oharai" or purification sumbolises the purification of the mind prior to prayer. On both sides of the great upper gate, deities holding bows and arrows stand on guard. At the stone wash basins, visitors wash their hands and mouth before praying at the main altar. In front of the offertory box they make a small monetary offering, then bow twice, clap twice and bow once again. The English language fortunes are the oldest English shrine fortunes in Japan and are still popular among visitors to Nagasaki. Fortunes are divided into "Best luck", "Average luck", "Good", "Half luck", "Partly bad luck", "Worst", "Bad luck but turns good", and "Luck in the end". Visitors tie bands of paper round the legs of the stone-carved guardian dogs and pray for help with things such as stopping smoking and succeeding in examinations.

'''Suwa Shinto shrine''' was constructed in [[1624]], after the [[Tokugawa Shogunate|Tokugawa]] [[Bakufu]] government had taken power and begun forcing [[Christians]] to reconvert to [[Buddhism]]. In [[1634]] a great [[autumn]] [[festival]] was held at the [[shrine]]. This marked the beginning of the famous [[Kunchi]] festival which continues to this day. Every year, thousands of Nagasaki citizens visit Suwa [[Shinto]] shrine to pray for a year of peace and prosperity. Passing under the huge entrance [[torii]] arch, and climbing the first flight of stone steps, the visitor notices a deity devoted to purification on the left. "Oharai" or purification sumbolises the purification of the mind prior to prayer. On both sides of the great upper gate, deities holding [[bows and arrows]] stand on guard. At the stone wash basins, visitors wash their hands and mouth before praying at the main [[altar]]. In front of the [[offertory]] box they make a small monetary offering, then bow twice, clap twice and bow once again. The English language fortunes are the oldest English shrine fortunes in Japan and are still popular among visitors to Nagasaki. Fortunes are divided into "Best luck", "Average luck", "Good", "Half luck", "Partly bad luck", "Worst", "Bad luck but turns good", and "Luck in the end". Visitors tie bands of paper round the legs of the stone-carved guardian [[dogs]] and pray for help with things such as stopping smoking and success in examinations.
{{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Nagasaki Prefecture]]

'''Suwa Shrine''' is the major shrine of [[Nagasaki]], [[Japan]], and home to the [[Kunchi|Kunchi festival]] . The shrine is dedicated to Suwa-no-Kami, a [[kami]] of valor and duty.
==History==
The official date of construction for Suwa Shrine is [[1614]], the same year as [[Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokygawa Ieyasu's]] Edict against Christianity, although there was little more than a small structure to mark the position of the future shrine at this time. At that time, the [[Tokugawa Shogunate|Tokugawa]] [[Bakufu]] government had taken power and reversed its [[Kirishitan#Japan.27s_policy_toward_Catholicism|friendly policy towards Christianity]]. The government had begun forcing [[Christians]] to reconvert to the Japanese religions of [[Shinbutsu shūgō|Buddhism and Shinto]].
The temporary stucture was frequently attacted by resisting Christians, until [[1624]] when Aoki Kensei came to Nagasaki. His religious zeal and skill at organizing, combined with authority granted by the leading Yoshida Shinto council, lead to the completion of the main structure of Suwa Shrine.
The opening of the shrine was attended by a ''yutate-sai'' ritual, where a priest demonstrates his communion with the kami by plunging his hands into boiling water unharmed. This ritual is still performed at Suwa Shrine today.
In [[1634]] a great [[autumn]] [[festival]] was held at the [[shrine]]. This marked the beginning of the famous [[Kunchi]] festival which continues to this day. Kunchi festival is held from [[October]] 7 to 9 every year and is considered one of the three major [[Japanese Festivals|festivals]] of [[Japan]], being designated as an Important Intangible Cultural Asset.
Every year, thousands of Nagasaki citizens visit Suwa [[Shinto]] shrine to pray for a year of peace and prosperity. Passing under the huge entrance [[torii]] arch, and climbing the first flight of stone steps, the visitor notices a deity devoted to purification on the left. "Oharai" or purification sumbolises the purification of the mind prior to prayer. On both sides of the great upper gate, deities holding [[bows and arrows]] stand on guard. At the stone wash basins, visitors wash their hands and mouth before praying at the main [[altar]]. In front of the [[offertory]] box they make a small monetary offering, then bow twice, clap twice and bow once again.
The English language fortunes are the oldest English shrine fortunes in Japan and are still popular among visitors to Nagasaki. Fortunes are divided into "Best luck", "Average luck", "Good", "Half luck", "Partly bad luck", "Worst", "Bad luck but turns good", and "Luck in the end".
Visitors tie bands of paper round the legs of the stone-carved guardian [[dogs]] and pray for help with things such as stopping smoking and success in examinations.
==References==
*Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8
{{Shinto-stub}}
{{coor title dms|32|45|16|N|129|52|54|E|region:JP-42_type:landmark_source:dewiki}}
[[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Nagasaki Prefecture]]

'''Suwa Shrine''' is the major shrine of [[Nagasaki]], [[Japan]], and home to the [[Kunchi|Kunchi festival]]. It is located in the northern part of the city, on the slopes of Mount Konpira. The shrine is dedicated to Suwa-no-Kami, a [[kami]] of valor and duty.
==History==
The official date of construction for Suwa Shrine is [[1614]], the same year as [[Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokygawa Ieyasu's]] Edict against Christianity, although there was little more than a small structure to mark the position of the future shrine at this time. At that time, the [[Tokugawa Shogunate|Tokugawa]] [[Bakufu]] government had taken power and reversed its [[Kirishitan#Japan.27s_policy_toward_Catholicism|friendly policy towards Christianity]]. The government had begun forcing [[Christians]] to reconvert to the Japanese religions of [[Shinbutsu shūgō|Buddhism and Shinto]].
The temporary stucture was frequently attacted by resisting Christians, until [[1624]] when Aoki Kensei came to Nagasaki. His religious zeal and skill at organizing, combined with authority granted by the leading Yoshida Shinto council, lead to the completion of the main structure of Suwa Shrine.
The opening of the shrine was attended by a ''yutate-sai'' ritual, where a priest demonstrates his communion with the kami by plunging his hands into boiling water unharmed. This ritual is still performed at Suwa Shrine today.
In [[1634]] a great [[autumn]] [[festival]] was held at the [[shrine]]. This marked the beginning of the famous [[Kunchi]] festival which continues to this day. Kunchi festival is held from [[October]] 7 to 9 every year and is considered one of the three major [[Japanese Festivals|festivals]] of [[Japan]], being designated as an Important Intangible Cultural Asset.
Suwa Shrine survived the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]] on [[August 9]], [[1945]]. It is thought to have survived intact to its strategic location in the central part of Mount Konpira's southern flank, although in the aftermath of the bomb local residents were quick to note that while the famous Urakai Cathedral and surrounding Catholic neighborhoods were obliterated, the Shinto shine still stood.
==Festivals==
Every year, thousands of Nagasaki citizens visit Suwa [[Shinto]] shrine to pray for a year of peace and prosperity. Passing under the huge entrance [[torii]] arch, and climbing the first flight of stone steps, the visitor notices a deity devoted to purification on the left. "Oharai" or purification sumbolises the purification of the mind prior to prayer. On both sides of the great upper gate, deities holding [[bows and arrows]] stand on guard. At the stone wash basins, visitors wash their hands and mouth before praying at the main [[altar]]. In front of the [[offertory]] box they make a small monetary offering, then bow twice, clap twice and bow once again.
The English language fortunes are the oldest English shrine fortunes in Japan and are still popular among visitors to Nagasaki. Fortunes are divided into "Best luck", "Average luck", "Good", "Half luck", "Partly bad luck", "Worst", "Bad luck but turns good", and "Luck in the end".
Visitors tie bands of paper round the legs of the stone-carved guardian [[dogs]] and pray for help with things such as stopping smoking and success in examinations.
==References==
*Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8

'''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is the major shrine of [[Nagasaki]], [[Japan]], and home to the [[Kunchi|Kunchi festival]]. It is located in the northern part of the city, on the slopes of Mount Konpira. The shrine is dedicated to Suwa-no-Kami, a [[kami]] of valor and duty.
==History==
The official date of construction for Suwa Shrine is [[1614]], the same year as [[Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokygawa Ieyasu's]] Edict against Christianity, although there was little more than a small structure to mark the position of the future shrine at this time. At that time, the [[Tokugawa Shogunate|Tokugawa]] [[Bakufu]] government had taken power and reversed its [[Kirishitan#Japan.27s_policy_toward_Catholicism|friendly policy towards Christianity]]. The government had begun forcing [[Christians]] to reconvert to the Japanese religions of [[Shinbutsu shūgō|Buddhism and Shinto]].
The temporary stucture was frequently attacted by resisting Christians, until [[1624]] when Aoki Kensei came to Nagasaki. His religious zeal and skill at organizing, combined with authority granted by the leading Yoshida Shinto council, lead to the completion of the main structure of Suwa Shrine.
The opening of the shrine was attended by a ''yutate-sai'' ritual, where a priest demonstrates his communion with the kami by plunging his hands into boiling water unharmed. This ritual is still performed at Suwa Shrine today.
In [[1634]] a great [[autumn]] [[festival]] was held at the [[shrine]]. This marked the beginning of the famous [[Kunchi]] festival which continues to this day. Kunchi festival is held from [[October]] 7 to 9 every year and is considered one of the three major [[Japanese Festivals|festivals]] of [[Japan]], being designated as an Important Intangible Cultural Asset.
Suwa Shrine survived the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]] on [[August 9]], [[1945]]. It is thought to have survived intact to its strategic location in the central part of Mount Konpira's southern flank, although in the aftermath of the bomb local residents were quick to note that while the famous Urakai Cathedral and surrounding Catholic neighborhoods were obliterated, the Shinto shine still stood.
==Festivals==
Like most [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]], every year thousands of people visit Suwa [[Shinto]] shrine to pray for peace and prosperity. Suwa shrine is also the host to many annual festivals. These festivals serve the joint purpose of honoring the kami, providing a sense of community for shrine worshipers, and providing exposure and income for the shrine itself.
* '''Okunchi''' - This festival is probably unique in all of Japan, in that it combines elements of Christian, Buddhist and Shinto worship to pray for the more than 70,000 people killed by the atomic bomb dropped on Nagasaki. It is an annual festival, taking place every year on August 9th.
* '''Doll's Festival''' - Like all of Japan, Suwa Shrine observes the [[Hina Matsuri|Doll's Festival]] on March 3rd. However, since the 1980s the festival has been observed in an unusual way, in that attractive, 20-year old women are selected to wear the shrine's collection of antique [[kimono]] and pose in a live Doll's Day procession.
* ''''''
The English language fortunes are the oldest English shrine fortunes in Japan and are still popular among visitors to Nagasaki. Fortunes are divided into "Best luck", "Average luck", "Good", "Half luck", "Partly bad luck", "Worst", "Bad luck but turns good", and "Luck in the end".
Visitors tie bands of paper round the legs of the stone-carved guardian [[dogs]] and pray for help with things such as stopping smoking and success in examinations.
==References==
*Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8

It is thought to have survived intact to its strategic location in the central part of Mount Konpira's southern flank, although in the aftermath of the bomb local residents were quick to note that while the famous Urakai Cathedral and surrounding Catholic neighborhoods were obliterated, the Shinto shine still stood.
==Festivals==
Like most [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]], every year thousands of people visit Suwa [[Shinto]] shrine to pray for peace and prosperity. Suwa shrine is also the host to many annual festivals. These festivals serve the joint purpose of honoring the kami, providing a sense of community for shrine worshipers, and providing exposure and income for the shrine itself.
* '''Atomic bomb commemorative service''' - This festival is probably unique in all of Japan, in that it combines elements of Christian, Buddhist and Shinto worship to pray for the more than 70,000 people killed by the atomic bomb dropped on Nagasaki. It is an annual festival, taking place every year on August 9th.
* '''Doll's Festival''' - Like all of Japan, Suwa Shrine observes the [[Hina Matsuri|Doll's Festival]] on March 3rd. However, since the 1980s the festival has been observed in an unusual way, in that attractive, 20-year old women are selected to wear the shrine's collection of antique [[kimono]] and pose in a live Doll's Day procession.
* '''[[Kunichi|Okunichi]]''' - The most famous of Suwa Shrine's festivals, this festival begain along with the founding of the shrine as a way to both demonstrate its new importance in the community and as a way to hunt for [[Kakure Kirishitan|Hidden Christians]]. It is now considered on of the three great festivals of Japan.
==English language fortunes==
The English language fortunes at Suwa shrine are the oldest English shrine fortunes in Japan and are still popular among visitors to Nagasaki. Fortunes are divided into "Best luck", "Average luck", "Good", "Half luck", "Partly bad luck", "Worst", "Bad luck but turns good", and "Luck in the end".
Visitors tie bands of paper round the legs of the stone-carved guardian [[dogs]] and pray for help with things such as stopping smoking and success in examinations.

In [[1634]] a great [[autumn]] [[festival]] was held at the [[shrine]]. This marked the beginning of the famous [[Kunchi]] festival which continues to this day. In [[1634]] a great [[autumn]] [[festival]] was held at the [[shrine]]. This marked the beginning of the famous [[Kunchi]] festival which continues to this day. Kunchi festival is held from [[October]] 7 to 9 every year and is considered one of the three major [[Japanese Festivals|festivals]] of [[Japan]], being designated as an Important Intangible Cultural Asset.
Suwa Shrine survived the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]] on [[August 9]], [[1945]]. It is thought to have survived intact to its strategic location in the central part of Mount Konpira's southern flank, although in the aftermath of the bomb local residents were quick to note that while the famous Urakai Cathedral and surrounding Catholic neighborhoods were obliterated, the Shinto shine still stood.
==Festivals==
Like most [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]], every year thousands of people visit Suwa [[Shinto]] shrine to pray for peace and prosperity. Suwa shrine is also the host to many annual festivals. These festivals serve the joint purpose of honoring the kami, providing a sense of community for shrine worshipers, and providing exposure and income for the shrine itself.
* '''Atomic bomb commemorative service''' - This festival is probably unique in all of Japan, in that it combines elements of Christian, Buddhist and Shinto worship to pray for the more than 70,000 people killed by the atomic bomb dropped on Nagasaki. It is an annual festival, taking place every year on August 9th.
* '''Doll's Festival''' - Like all of Japan, Suwa Shrine observes the [[Hina Matsuri|Doll's Festival]] on March 3rd. However, since the 1980s the festival has been observed in an unusual way, in that attractive, 20-year old women are selected to wear the shrine's collection of antique [[kimono]] and pose in a live Doll's Day procession.
* '''[[Kunchi|Okunchi]]''' - The most famous of Suwa Shrine's festivals, this festival begain along with the founding of the shrine as a way to both demonstrate its new importance in the community and as a way to hunt for [[Kakure Kirishitan|Hidden Christians]]. It is now considered on of the three great festivals of Japan.
==English language fortunes==
Most Shinto shrines sell Omikuchi, a type of fortune telling that comes on a slip of paper that lists your particular luck at that time. Suwa shrine was the first shrine in Japan to offer Omikuchi in English.
The fortunes are divided into:
* Best luck * Average luck * Good luck * Half luck * Partly bad luck * Worst luck * Bad luck but turns good * Luck in the end
Visitors tie bands of paper round the legs of the stone-carved [[Imperial guardian lions|guardian lions]] and pray for help with things such as [[Smoking cessation|stopping smoking]] and [[Entrance examination|success in examinations.]]

The temporary stucture was frequently attacted by resisting Christians, until [[1624]] when Aoki Kensei came to Nagasaki. His religious zeal and skill at organizing, combined with authority granted by the leading Yoshida Shinto council, lead to the completion of the main structure of Suwa Shrine.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
The opening of the shrine was attended by a ''yutate-sai'' ritual, where a priest demonstrates his communion with the kami by plunging his hands into boiling water unharmed. This ritual is still performed at Suwa Shrine today.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
In [[1634]] a great [[autumn]] [[festival]] was held at the [[shrine]]. This marked the beginning of the famous [[Kunchi]] festival which continues to this day. Kunchi festival is held from [[October]] 7 to 9 every year and is considered one of the three major [[Japanese Festivals|festivals]] of [[Japan]], being designated as an Important Intangible Cultural Asset.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
Suwa Shrine survived the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]] on [[August 9]], [[1945]]. It is thought to have survived intact to its strategic location in the central part of Mount Konpira's southern flank, although in the aftermath of the bomb local residents were quick to note that while the famous [[Urakami Cathedral]] and surrounding Catholic neighborhoods were obliterated, the Shinto shine still stood.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
==Festivals==
Like most [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]], every year thousands of people visit Suwa [[Shinto]] shrine to pray for peace and prosperity. Suwa shrine is also the host to many annual festivals. These festivals serve the joint purpose of honoring the kami, providing a sense of community for shrine worshipers, and providing exposure and income for the shrine itself.
* '''Atomic bomb commemorative service''' - This festival is probably unique in all of Japan, in that it combines elements of Christian, Buddhist and Shinto worship to pray for the more than 70,000 people killed by the atomic bomb dropped on Nagasaki. It is an annual festival, taking place every year on August 9th.
* '''Doll's Festival''' - Like all of Japan, Suwa Shrine observes the [[Hina Matsuri|Doll's Festival]] on March 3rd. However, since the 1980s the festival has been observed in an unusual way, in that attractive, 20-year old women are selected to wear the shrine's collection of antique [[kimono]] and pose in a live Doll's Day procession.
* '''[[Kunchi|Okunchi]]''' - The most famous of Suwa Shrine's festivals, this festival begain along with the founding of the shrine as a way to both demonstrate its new importance in the community and as a way to hunt for [[Kakure Kirishitan|Hidden Christians]]. It is now considered on of the three great festivals of Japan.
==English language fortunes==
Most Shinto shrines sell Omikuchi, a type of fortune telling that comes on a slip of paper that lists your particular luck at that time. Suwa shrine was the first shrine in Japan to offer Omikuchi in English.
The fortunes are divided into:
* Best luck * Average luck * Good luck * Half luck * Partly bad luck * Worst luck * Bad luck but turns good * Luck in the end
Visitors tie bands of paper round the legs of the stone-carved [[Imperial guardian lions|guardian lions]] and pray for help with things such as [[Smoking cessation|stopping smoking]] and [[Entrance examination|success in examinations.]]

'''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is the major [[Shinto shrine]] of [[Nagasaki]], [[Japan]], and home to the [[Kunchi|Kunchi festival]]. It is located in the northern part of the city, on the slopes of Mount Konpira. The shrine is dedicated to Suwa-no-Kami, a [[kami]] of valor and duty.
While most Shinto shrines have a traditional and spiritual story of foundation, Suwa shrine is fairly unique in that it was established for political reasons, as a way of stopping and reverting the conversion to Christianity that had swept over much of Nagasaki.
==History==
The official date of construction for Suwa Shrine is [[1614]], the same year as [[Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokygawa Ieyasu's]] Edict against Christianity, although there was little more than a small structure to mark the position of the future shrine at this time. At that time, Nagasaki was home to the largest Christian population in Japan, and had destroyed many of the former Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples. The [[Tokugawa Shogunate|Tokugawa]] [[Bakufu]] government had taken power and reversed its [[Kirishitan#Japan.27s_policy_toward_Catholicism|friendly policy towards Christianity]]. The government had begun forcing [[Christians]] to reconvert to the Japanese religions of [[Shinbutsu shūgō|Buddhism and Shinto]]. It was thought that establishing a major point of Shinto worship would be important to these efforts, giving the local population a central point of worship and a sense of community.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
The temporary stucture was frequently attacted by resisting Christians, until [[1624]] when Aoki Kensei came to Nagasaki. His religious zeal and skill at organizing, combined with authority granted by the leading Yoshida Shinto council, lead to the completion of the main structure of Suwa Shrine.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
The opening of the shrine was attended by a ''yutate-sai'' ritual, where a priest demonstrates his communion with the kami by plunging his hands into boiling water unharmed. This ritual is still performed at Suwa Shrine today.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
In [[1634]] a great [[autumn]] [[festival]] was held at the [[shrine]]. This marked the beginning of the famous [[Kunchi]] festival which continues to this day. Kunchi festival is held from [[October]] 7 to 9 every year and is considered one of the three major [[Japanese Festivals|festivals]] of [[Japan]], being designated as an Important Intangible Cultural Asset.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
Suwa Shrine survived the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]] on [[August 9]], [[1945]]. It is thought to have survived intact to its strategic location in the central part of Mount Konpira's southern flank, although in the aftermath of the bomb local residents were quick to note that while the famous [[Urakami Cathedral]] and surrounding Catholic neighborhoods were obliterated, the Shinto shine still stood. Priests from the shrine took an active role in the rebuilding of Nagasaki, including consecrating the land and purifying structures that still stood.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
==Festivals==
Like most [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]], every year thousands of people visit Suwa [[Shinto]] shrine to pray for peace and prosperity. Suwa shrine is also the host to many annual festivals. These festivals serve the joint purpose of honoring the kami, providing a sense of community for shrine worshipers, and providing exposure and income for the shrine itself.
* '''Atomic bomb commemorative service''' - This festival is probably unique in all of Japan, in that it combines elements of Christian, Buddhist and Shinto worship to pray for the more than 70,000 people killed by the atomic bomb dropped on Nagasaki. It is an annual festival, taking place every year on August 9th.
* '''Doll's Festival''' - Like all of Japan, Suwa Shrine observes the [[Hina Matsuri|Doll's Festival]] on March 3rd. However, since the 1980s the festival has been observed in an unusual way, in that attractive, 20-year old women are selected to wear the shrine's collection of antique [[kimono]] and pose in a live Doll's Day procession.
* '''[[Kunchi|Okunchi]]''' - The most famous of Suwa Shrine's festivals, this festival begain along with the founding of the shrine as a way to both demonstrate its new importance in the community and as a way to hunt for [[Kakure Kirishitan|Hidden Christians]]. It is now considered on of the three great festivals of Japan.
==English language fortunes==
Most Shinto shrines sell Omikuchi, a type of fortune telling that comes on a slip of paper that lists your particular luck at that time. Suwa shrine was the first shrine in Japan to offer Omikuchi in English.
The fortunes are divided into:
* Best luck * Average luck * Good luck * Half luck * Partly bad luck * Worst luck * Bad luck but turns good * Luck in the end
Visitors tie bands of paper round the legs of the stone-carved [[Imperial guardian lions|guardian lions]] and pray for help with things such as [[Smoking cessation|stopping smoking]] and [[Entrance examination|success in examinations.]]
==References==
<div class="references-2column"><references/>
==Further Reading==
*Nelson, John K. [http://www.uwosh.edu/faculty_staff/earns/suwa.html Historical monuments at Nagasaki Suwa shrine]
{{coor title dms|32|45|16|N|129|52|54|E|region:JP-42_type:landmark_source:dewiki}}
[[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki]]

'''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is the major [[Shinto shrine]] of [[Nagasaki]], [[Japan]], and home to the [[Kunchi|Kunchi festival]]. It is located in the northern part of the city, on the slopes of Mount Konpira. The shrine is dedicated to Suwa-no-Kami, a [[kami]] of valor and duty.
While most Shinto shrines have a traditional and spiritual story of foundation, Suwa shrine is fairly unique in that it was established for political reasons, as a way of stopping and reverting the conversion to Christianity that had swept over much of Nagasaki. In modern times it remains an important and succesful center of the community.
==History==
The official date of construction for Suwa Shrine is [[1614]], the same year as [[Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokygawa Ieyasu's]] Edict against Christianity, although there was little more than a small structure to mark the position of the future shrine at this time. At that time, Nagasaki was home to the largest Christian population in Japan, and had destroyed many of the former Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples. The [[Tokugawa Shogunate|Tokugawa]] [[Bakufu]] government had taken power and reversed its [[Kirishitan#Japan.27s_policy_toward_Catholicism|friendly policy towards Christianity]]. The government had begun forcing [[Christians]] to reconvert to the Japanese religions of [[Shinbutsu shūgō|Buddhism and Shinto]]. It was thought that establishing a major point of Shinto worship would be important to these efforts, giving the local population a central point of worship and a sense of community.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
The temporary stucture was frequently attacted by resisting Christians, until [[1624]] when Aoki Kensei came to Nagasaki. His religious zeal and skill at organizing, combined with authority granted by the leading Yoshida Shinto council, lead to the completion of the main structure of Suwa Shrine.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
The opening of the shrine was attended by a ''yutate-sai'' ritual, where a priest demonstrates his communion with the kami by plunging his hands into boiling water unharmed. This ritual is still performed at Suwa Shrine today.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
In [[1634]] a great [[autumn]] [[festival]] was held at the [[shrine]]. This marked the beginning of the famous [[Kunchi]] festival which continues to this day. Kunchi festival is held from [[October]] 7 to 9 every year and is considered one of the three major [[Japanese Festivals|festivals]] of [[Japan]], being designated as an Important Intangible Cultural Asset.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
Suwa Shrine survived the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]] on [[August 9]], [[1945]]. It is thought to have survived intact to its strategic location in the central part of Mount Konpira's southern flank, although in the aftermath of the bomb local residents were quick to note that while the famous [[Urakami Cathedral]] and surrounding Catholic neighborhoods were obliterated, the Shinto shine still stood. Priests from the shrine took an active role in the rebuilding of Nagasaki, including consecrating the land and purifying structures that still stood.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
==Festivals==
Like most [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]], every year thousands of people visit Suwa [[Shinto]] shrine to pray for peace and prosperity. Suwa shrine is also the host to many annual festivals. These festivals serve the joint purpose of honoring the kami, providing a sense of community for shrine worshipers, and providing exposure and income for the shrine itself.
* '''Atomic bomb commemorative service''' - This festival is probably unique in all of Japan, in that it combines elements of Christian, Buddhist and Shinto worship to pray for the more than 70,000 people killed by the atomic bomb dropped on Nagasaki. It is an annual festival, taking place every year on August 9th.
* '''Doll's Festival''' - Like all of Japan, Suwa Shrine observes the [[Hina Matsuri|Doll's Festival]] on March 3rd. However, since the 1980s the festival has been observed in an unusual way, in that attractive, 20-year old women are selected to wear the shrine's collection of antique [[kimono]] and pose in a live Doll's Day procession.
* '''[[Kunchi|Okunchi]]''' - The most famous of Suwa Shrine's festivals, this festival begain along with the founding of the shrine as a way to both demonstrate its new importance in the community and as a way to hunt for [[Kakure Kirishitan|Hidden Christians]]. It is now considered on of the three great festivals of Japan.
==English language fortunes==
Most Shinto shrines sell Omikuchi, a type of fortune telling that comes on a slip of paper that lists your particular luck at that time. Suwa shrine was the first shrine in Japan to offer Omikuchi in English.
The fortunes are divided into:
* Best luck * Average luck * Good luck * Half luck * Partly bad luck * Worst luck * Bad luck but turns good * Luck in the end
Visitors tie bands of paper round the legs of the stone-carved [[Imperial guardian lions|guardian lions]] and pray for help with things such as [[Smoking cessation|stopping smoking]] and [[Entrance examination|success in examinations.]]
==References==
<div class="references-2column"><references/>
==Further Reading==
*Nelson, John K. [http://www.uwosh.edu/faculty_staff/earns/suwa.html Historical monuments at Nagasaki Suwa shrine]

It was thought that establishing a major point of Shinto worship would be important to these efforts, giving the local population a central point of worship and a sense of community.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
The temporary stucture was frequently attacted by resisting Christians, until [[1624]] when Aoki Kensei came to Nagasaki. His religious zeal and skill at organizing, combined with authority granted by the leading Yoshida Shinto council, lead to the completion of the main structure of Suwa Shrine.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref> The opening of the shrine was attended by a ''yutate-sai'' ritual, where a priest demonstrates his communion with the kami by plunging his hands into boiling water unharmed. It has been designated as an Important Intangible Cultural Asset.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>

It is thought to have survived intact to its strategic location in the central part of Mount Konpira's southern flank, although in the aftermath of the bomb local residents were quick to note that while the famous [[Urakami Cathedral]] and surrounding Catholic neighborhoods were obliterated, the Shinto shine still stood. Priests from the shrine took an active role in the rebuilding of Nagasaki, including consecrating the land and purifying structures that still stood.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
==Festivals==

However, since the 1980s the festival has been observed in an unusual way, in that attractive, 20-year old women are selected to wear the shrine's collection of antique [[kimono]] and pose in a live Doll's Day procession.
* '''[[Kunchi|Okunchi]]''' - The most famous of Suwa Shrine's festivals, this festival begain along with the founding of the shrine as a way to both demonstrate its new importance in the community and as a way to hunt for [[Kakure Kirishitan|Hidden Christians]]. It is now considered on of the three great festivals of Japan.
==English language fortunes==
Most Shinto shrines sell Omikuchi, a type of fortune telling that comes on a slip of paper that lists your particular luck at that time. Suwa shrine was the first shrine in Japan to offer Omikuchi in English.

* Best luck * Average luck * Good luck * Half luck * Partly bad luck * Worst luck * Bad luck but turns good * Luck in the end

Suwa Shrine survived the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]] on [[August 9]], [[1945]]. It is thought to have survived intact to its strategic location in the central part of Mount Konpira's southern flank, although in the aftermath of the bomb local residents were quick to note that while the famous [[Urakami Cathedral]] and surrounding Catholic neighborhoods were obliterated, the Shinto shine still stood. Priests from the shrine took an active role in the rebuilding of Nagasaki, including consecrating the land and purifying structures that still stood.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
==Festivals==
Like most [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]], every year thousands of people visit Suwa [[Shinto]] shrine to pray for peace and prosperity. Suwa shrine is also the host to many annual festivals. These festivals serve the joint purpose of honoring the kami, providing a sense of community for shrine worshipers, and providing exposure and income for the shrine itself.
* '''Atomic bomb commemorative service''' - This festival is probably unique in all of Japan, in that it combines elements of Christian, Buddhist and Shinto worship to pray for the more than 70,000 people killed by the atomic bomb dropped on Nagasaki. It is an annual festival, taking place every year on August 9th.
* '''Doll's Festival''' - Like all of Japan, Suwa Shrine observes the [[Hina Matsuri|Doll's Festival]] on March 3rd. However, since the 1980s the festival has been observed in an unusual way, in that attractive, 20-year old women are selected to wear the shrine's collection of antique [[kimono]] and pose in a live Doll's Day procession.
* '''[[Kunchi|Okunchi]]''' - The most famous of Suwa Shrine's festivals, this festival begain along with the founding of the shrine as a way to both demonstrate its new importance in the community and as a way to hunt for [[Kakure Kirishitan|Hidden Christians]]. It is now considered on of the three great festivals of Japan.
==English language fortunes==
Most Shinto shrines sell Omikuji, a type of fortune telling that comes on a slip of paper that lists your particular luck at that time. Suwa shrine was the first shrine in Japan to offer Omikuchi in English.
The fortunes are divided into:
* Best luck * Average luck * Good luck * Half luck * Partly bad luck * Worst luck * Bad luck but turns good * Luck in the end
Visitors tie bands of paper round the legs of the stone-carved [[Imperial guardian lions|guardian lions]] and pray for help with things such as [[Smoking cessation|stopping smoking]] and [[Entrance examination|success in examinations.]]
==References== <div class="references"><references/>
==Further Reading==
*Nelson, John K. [http://www.uwosh.edu/faculty_staff/earns/suwa.html Historical monuments at Nagasaki Suwa shrine]

[[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki]]
[[de:Suwa-Schrein (Nagasaki)]] [[ja:鎮西大社諏訪神社]]

'''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is the major [[Shinto shrine]] of [[Nagasaki]], [[Japan]], and home to the [[Kunchi|Kunchi festival]]. It is located in the northern part of the city, on the slopes of Mount Konpira. The shrine is dedicated to Suwa-no-Kami, a [[kami]] of valor and duty.
While most Shinto shrines have a traditional and spiritual story of foundation, Suwa shrine is fairly unique in that it was established for political reasons, as a way of stopping and reverting the conversion to Christianity that had swept over much of Nagasaki. In modern times it remains an important and succesful center of the community.
==History==
The official date of construction for Suwa Shrine is [[1614]], the same year as [[Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokygawa Ieyasu's]] Edict against Christianity, although there was little more than a small structure to mark the position of the future shrine at this time. At that time, Nagasaki was home to the largest Christian population in Japan, and had destroyed many of the former Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples. The [[Tokugawa Shogunate|Tokugawa]] [[Bakufu]] government had taken power and reversed its [[Kirishitan#Japan.27s_policy_toward_Catholicism|friendly policy towards Christianity]]. The government had begun forcing [[Christians]] to reconvert to the Japanese religions of [[Shinbutsu shūgō|Buddhism and Shinto]]. It was thought that establishing a major point of Shinto worship would be important to these efforts, giving the local population a central point of worship and a sense of community.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
The temporary stucture was frequently attacted by resisting Christians, until [[1624]] when Aoki Kensei came to Nagasaki. His religious zeal and skill at organizing, combined with authority granted by the leading Yoshida Shinto council, lead to the completion of the main structure of Suwa Shrine.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref> The opening of the shrine was attended by a ''yutate-sai'' ritual, where a priest demonstrates his communion with the kami by plunging his hands into boiling water unharmed. This unusual ritual is still performed at Suwa Shrine today, although is rare at other shrines. <ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
In [[1634]], in order to further increase local participation in shrine events, a great [[Japanese festivals|autumn estival]] was held at the [[shrine]]. This marked the beginning of the famous [[Kunchi]] festival which continues to this day. Kunchi festival is held from [[October]] 7 to 9 every year and is considered one of the major festivals of [[Japan]], along with the [[Gion Matsuri]] and Osaka's Tenjin Matsuri. It has been designated as an Important Intangible Cultural Asset.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
Suwa Shrine survived the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]] on [[August 9]], [[1945]]. It is thought to have survived intact to its strategic location in the central part of Mount Konpira's southern flank, although in the aftermath of the bomb local residents were quick to note that while the famous [[Urakami Cathedral]] and surrounding Catholic neighborhoods were obliterated, the Shinto shine still stood. Priests from the shrine took an active role in the rebuilding of Nagasaki, including consecrating the land and purifying structures that still stood.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
==Festivals==
Like most [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]], every year thousands of people visit Suwa [[Shinto]] shrine to pray for peace and prosperity. The Shrine is also the main destination in Nagasaki for festivals such as [[Shichi-Go-San]] and [[Seijin Shiki]].
Suwa shrine is also the host to many annual festivals. These festivals serve the joint purpose of honoring the kami, providing a sense of community for shrine worshipers, and providing exposure and income for the shrine itself. Some of these festivals are typical to all major Shinto shrines in Japan, but some are unique to Suwa shrine.
* '''Atomic bomb commemorative service''' - This festival is probably unique in all of Japan, in that it combines elements of Christian, Buddhist and Shinto worship to pray for the more than 70,000 people killed by the atomic bomb dropped on Nagasaki. It is an annual festival, taking place every year on August 9th.
* '''Doll's Festival''' - Like all of Japan, Suwa Shrine observes the [[Hina Matsuri|Doll's Festival]] on March 3rd. However, since the 1980s the festival has been observed in an unusual way, in that attractive, 20-year old women are selected to wear the shrine's collection of antique [[kimono]] and pose in a live Doll's Day procession. The competition amongst the young women is fierce, and there are far more applicants than positions in the festival. The two most beautiful women are chosen for the key roles of the Emperor and Emperess in the procession. This tradition was created largely as a [[media event]] to promote and advertise Suwa shrine, and every year television crews are invited to broadcast the festival.
* '''[[Kunchi|Okunchi]]''' - The most famous of Suwa Shrine's festivals, this festival begain along with the founding of the shrine as a way to both demonstrate its new importance in the community and as a way to hunt for [[Kakure Kirishitan|Hidden Christians]]. It is now considered on of the three great festivals of Japan.
==English language fortunes==
Most Shinto shrines sell Omikuji, a type of fortune telling that comes on a slip of paper that lists your particular luck at that time. Suwa shrine was the first shrine in Japan to offer Omikuchi in English.
The fortunes are divided into:
* Best luck * Average luck * Good luck * Half luck * Partly bad luck * Worst luck * Bad luck but turns good * Luck in the end
Visitors tie bands of paper round the legs of the stone-carved [[Imperial guardian lions|guardian lions]] and pray for help with things such as [[Smoking cessation|stopping smoking]] and [[Entrance examination|success in examinations.]]
==References== <div class="references"><references/>
==Further Reading==
*Nelson, John K. [http://www.uwosh.edu/faculty_staff/earns/suwa.html Historical monuments at Nagasaki Suwa shrine]

[[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki]]
[[de:Suwa-Schrein (Nagasaki)]] [[ja:鎮西大社諏訪神社]]

'''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is the major [[Shinto shrine]] of [[Nagasaki]], [[Japan]], and home to the [[Kunchi|Kunchi festival]]. It is located in the northern part of the city, on the slopes of Mount Konpira. The shrine is dedicated to Suwa-no-Kami, a [[kami]] of valor and duty.
While most Shinto shrines have a traditional and spiritual story of foundation, Suwa shrine is fairly unique in that it was established for political reasons, as a way of stopping and reverting the conversion to Christianity that had swept over much of Nagasaki. In modern times it remains an important and succesful center of the community.
==History==
The official date of construction for Suwa Shrine is [[1614]], the same year as [[Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokygawa Ieyasu's]] Edict against Christianity, although there was little more than a small structure to mark the position of the future shrine at this time. At that time, Nagasaki was home to the largest Christian population in Japan, and had destroyed many of the former Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples. The [[Tokugawa Shogunate|Tokugawa]] [[Bakufu]] government had taken power and reversed its [[Kirishitan#Japan.27s_policy_toward_Catholicism|friendly policy towards Christianity]]. The government had begun forcing [[Christians]] to reconvert to the Japanese religions of [[Shinbutsu shūgō|Buddhism and Shinto]]. It was thought that establishing a major point of Shinto worship would be important to these efforts, giving the local population a central point of worship and a sense of community.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
The temporary stucture was frequently attacted by resisting Christians, until [[1624]] when Aoki Kensei came to Nagasaki. His religious zeal and skill at organizing, combined with authority granted by the leading Yoshida Shinto council, lead to the completion of the main structure of Suwa Shrine.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref> The opening of the shrine was attended by a ''yutate-sai'' ritual, where a priest demonstrates his communion with the kami by plunging his hands into boiling water unharmed. This unusual ritual is still performed at Suwa Shrine today, although is rare at other shrines. <ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
In [[1634]], in order to further increase local participation in shrine events, a great [[Japanese festivals|autumn festival]] was held at the [[shrine]]. This marked the beginning of the famous [[Kunchi]] festival which continues to this day. Kunchi festival is held from [[October]] 7 to 9 every year and is considered one of the major festivals of [[Japan]], along with the [[Gion Matsuri]] and Osaka's Tenjin Matsuri. It has been designated as an Important Intangible Cultural Asset.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
Suwa Shrine survived the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]] on [[August 9]], [[1945]]. It is thought to have survived intact to its strategic location in the central part of Mount Konpira's southern flank, although in the aftermath of the bomb local residents were quick to note that while the famous [[Urakami Cathedral]] and surrounding Catholic neighborhoods were obliterated, the Shinto shine still stood.
This was considered to be signifigant by the survivers of the bombing, showing the power of the native Japanese kami as opposed to the imported Christian god. In addition, priests from Suwa shrine took an active role in the rebuilding of Nagasaki, including consecrating the land and purifying structures that still stood.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
==Festivals==
Like most [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]], every year thousands of people visit Suwa [[Shinto]] shrine to pray for peace and prosperity. The Shrine is also the main destination in Nagasaki for festivals such as [[Shichi-Go-San]] and [[Seijin Shiki]].
Suwa shrine is also the host to many annual festivals. These festivals serve the joint purpose of honoring the kami, providing a sense of community for shrine worshipers, and providing exposure and income for the shrine itself. Some of these festivals are typical to all major Shinto shrines in Japan, but some are unique to Suwa shrine.
* '''Atomic bomb commemorative service''' - This festival is probably unique in all of Japan, in that it combines elements of Christian, Buddhist and Shinto worship to pray for the more than 70,000 people killed by the atomic bomb dropped on Nagasaki. It is an annual festival, taking place every year on August 9th.
* '''Doll's Festival''' - Like all of Japan, Suwa Shrine observes the [[Hina Matsuri|Doll's Festival]] on March 3rd. However, since the 1980s the festival has been observed in an unusual way, in that attractive, 20-year old women are selected to wear the shrine's collection of antique [[kimono]] and pose in a live Doll's Day procession. The competition amongst the young women is fierce, and there are far more applicants than positions in the festival. The two most beautiful women are chosen for the key roles of the Emperor and Emperess in the procession. This tradition was created largely as a [[media event]] to promote and advertise Suwa shrine, and every year television crews are invited to broadcast the festival.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
* '''[[Kunchi|Okunchi]]''' - The most famous of Suwa Shrine's festivals, this festival begain along with the founding of the shrine as a way to both demonstrate its new importance in the community and as a way to hunt for [[Kakure Kirishitan|Hidden Christians]]. It is now considered on of the three great festivals of Japan.
==English language fortunes==
Most Shinto shrines sell Omikuji, a type of fortune telling that comes on a slip of paper that lists your particular luck at that time. Suwa shrine was the first shrine in Japan to offer Omikuchi in English.
The fortunes are divided into:
* Best luck * Average luck * Good luck * Half luck * Partly bad luck * Worst luck * Bad luck but turns good * Luck in the end
Visitors tie bands of paper round the legs of the stone-carved [[Imperial guardian lions|guardian lions]] and pray for help with things such as [[Smoking cessation|stopping smoking]] and [[Entrance examination|success in examinations.]]
==References== <div class="references"><references/>
==Further Reading==
*Nelson, John K. [http://www.uwosh.edu/faculty_staff/earns/suwa.html Historical monuments at Nagasaki Suwa shrine]

The [[Tokugawa Shogunate|Tokugawa]] [[Bakufu]] government had taken power and reversed its [[Kirishitan#Japan.27s_policy_toward_Catholicism|friendly policy towards Christianity]]. The government had begun forcing [[Christians]] to reconvert to the Japanese religions of [[Shinbutsu shūgō|Buddhism and Shinto]]. It was thought that establishing a major point of Shinto worship would be important to these efforts, giving the local population a central point of worship and a sense of community.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
The temporary stucture was frequently attacted by resisting Christians, until [[1624]] when Aoki Kensei came to Nagasaki. His religious zeal and skill at organizing, combined with authority granted by the leading Yoshida Shinto council, lead to the completion of the main structure of Suwa Shrine.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref> In order to attract attention and encourage attendence at the new shrine, a dramatic ''yutate-sai'' ritual, where a priest demonstrates his communion with the kami by plunging his hands into boiling water unharmed, was performed. In spite of this, as well as the [[Sumo|sumo match]] that followed, very few people came to watch.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
In [[1634]], in another attempt to further increase local participation in shrine events, a great [[Japanese festivals|autumn festival]] was held at the [[shrine]]. This marked the beginning of the famous [[Kunchi]] festival which continues to this day. Kunchi festival is held from [[October]] 7 to 9 every year and is considered one of the major festivals of [[Japan]], along with the [[Gion Matsuri]] and Osaka's Tenjin Matsuri. It has been designated as an Important Intangible Cultural Asset.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
Suwa Shrine survived the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]] on [[August 9]], [[1945]]. It is thought to have survived intact to its strategic location in the central part of Mount Konpira's southern flank, although in the aftermath of the bomb local residents were quick to note that while the famous [[Urakami Cathedral]] and surrounding Catholic neighborhoods were obliterated, the Shinto shine still stood.
This was considered to be signifigant by the survivers of the bombing, showing the power of the native Japanese kami as opposed to the imported Christian god. In addition, priests from Suwa shrine took an active role in the rebuilding of Nagasaki, including consecrating the land and purifying structures that still stood.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
==Festivals==
Like most [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]], every year thousands of people visit Suwa [[Shinto]] shrine to pray for peace and prosperity. The Shrine is also the main destination in Nagasaki for festivals such as [[Shichi-Go-San]] and [[Seijin Shiki]].
Suwa shrine is also the host to many annual festivals. These festivals serve the joint purpose of honoring the kami, providing a sense of community for shrine worshipers, and providing exposure and income for the shrine itself. Some of these festivals are typical to all major Shinto shrines in Japan, but some are unique to Suwa shrine.
* '''Atomic bomb commemorative service''' - This festival is probably unique in all of Japan, in that it combines elements of Christian, Buddhist and Shinto worship to pray for the more than 70,000 people killed by the atomic bomb dropped on Nagasaki. It is an annual festival, taking place every year on August 9th.
* '''Doll's Festival''' - Like all of Japan, Suwa Shrine observes the [[Hina Matsuri|Doll's Festival]] on March 3rd. However, since the 1980s the festival has been observed in an unusual way, in that attractive, 20-year old women are selected to wear the shrine's collection of antique [[kimono]] and pose in a live Doll's Day procession. The competition amongst the young women is fierce, and there are far more applicants than positions in the festival. The two most beautiful women are chosen for the key roles of the Emperor and Emperess in the procession. This tradition was created largely as a [[media event]] to promote and advertise Suwa shrine, and every year television crews are invited to broadcast the festival.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
* '''[[Kunchi|Okunchi]]''' - The most famous of Suwa Shrine's festivals, this festival begain along with the founding of the shrine as a way to both demonstrate its new importance in the community and as a way to hunt for [[Kakure Kirishitan|Hidden Christians]]. Kunchi festival is held from [[October]] 7 to 9 every year and is considered one of the major festivals of [[Japan]], along with the [[Gion Matsuri]] and Osaka's Tenjin Matsuri. It has been designated as an Important Intangible Cultural Asset.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
* '''Yutate-sai''' - A theatrical ritual, originally performed by [[Yamabushi]], a priest demonstrates his communion with the kami by plunging his hands into boiling water unharmed. This ritual was performed on the opening of Suwa shrine, and is continued to be performed to this day. It is very rare at other Shinto shrines
==English language fortunes==
Most Shinto shrines sell Omikuji, a type of fortune telling that comes on a slip of paper that lists your particular luck at that time. Suwa shrine was the first shrine in Japan to offer Omikuchi in English.
The fortunes are divided into:
* Best luck * Average luck * Good luck * Half luck * Partly bad luck * Worst luck * Bad luck but turns good * Luck in the end
Visitors tie bands of paper round the legs of the stone-carved [[Imperial guardian lions|guardian lions]] and pray for help with things such as [[Smoking cessation|stopping smoking]] and [[Entrance examination|success in examinations.]]
==References== <div class="references"><references/>
==Further Reading==
*Nelson, John K. [http://www.uwosh.edu/faculty_staff/earns/suwa.html Historical monuments at Nagasaki Suwa shrine]

[[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki]]
[[de:Suwa-Schrein (Nagasaki)]] [[ja:鎮西大社諏訪神社]]

'''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is the major [[Shinto shrine]] of [[Nagasaki]], [[Japan]], and home to the [[Kunchi|Kunchi festival]]. It is located in the northern part of the city, on the slopes of Mount Konpira. The shrine is dedicated to Suwa-no-Kami, a [[kami]] of valor and duty.
While most Shinto shrines have a traditional and spiritual story of foundation, Suwa shrine is fairly unique in that it was established for political reasons, as a way of stopping and reverting the conversion to Christianity that had swept over much of Nagasaki. In modern times it remains an important and succesful center of the community.
==History==
The official date of construction for Suwa Shrine is [[1614]], the same year as [[Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokygawa Ieyasu's]] Edict against Christianity, although there was little more than a small structure to mark the position of the future shrine at this time. At that time, Nagasaki was home to the largest Christian population in Japan, and had destroyed many of the former Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples. The [[Tokugawa Shogunate|Tokugawa]] [[Bakufu]] government had taken power and reversed its [[Kirishitan#Japan.27s_policy_toward_Catholicism|friendly policy towards Christianity]]. The government had begun forcing [[Christians]] to reconvert to the Japanese religions of [[Shinbutsu shūgō|Buddhism and Shinto]]. It was thought that establishing a major point of Shinto worship would be important to these efforts, giving the local population a central point of worship and a sense of community.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
The temporary stucture was frequently attacted by resisting Christians, until [[1624]] when Aoki Kensei came to Nagasaki. His religious zeal and skill at organizing, combined with authority granted by the leading Yoshida Shinto council, lead to the completion of the main structure of Suwa Shrine.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref> In order to attract attention and encourage attendence at the new shrine, a dramatic ''yutate-sai'' ritual, where a priest demonstrates his communion with the kami by plunging his hands into boiling water unharmed, was performed. In spite of this, as well as the [[Sumo|sumo match]] that followed, very few people came to watch.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
In [[1634]], an edict was issued requiring all local people to register at the shrine and be counted. In addition to this, in another attempt to further increase local participation in shrine events, a great [[Japanese festivals|autumn festival]] was held at the [[shrine]]. Both of these events were created in the attempt to discover any remaining Christians, who would not be able paricipate in the festival or register at the shrine. Anyone who failed to do so was arrested, tortured and possibly executed if they did not renounce their Christian faith.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
Because Nagasaki was the only place in Japan with an [[Dejima|open port]], it was considered essential to impress the Dutch and Chinese traders with Japanese culture. In addition to the grand festival, from [[1638]] major performances of [[Noh]] were also held at Suwa shrine, at the direct command of the Shogun. These performances continued until [[1856]], when a major fire at the shrine destroyed most of the expensive Noh masks and props.
Suwa Shrine survived the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]] on [[August 9]], [[1945]]. It is thought to have survived intact to its strategic location in the central part of Mount Konpira's southern flank, although in the aftermath of the bomb local residents were quick to note that while the famous [[Urakami Cathedral]] and surrounding Catholic neighborhoods were obliterated, the Shinto shine still stood.
This was considered to be signifigant by the survivers of the bombing, showing the power of the native Japanese kami as opposed to the imported Christian god. In addition, priests from Suwa shrine took an active role in the rebuilding of Nagasaki, including consecrating the land and purifying structures that still stood.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
==Festivals==
Like most [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]], every year thousands of people visit Suwa [[Shinto]] shrine to pray for peace and prosperity. The Shrine is also the main destination in Nagasaki for festivals such as [[Shichi-Go-San]] and [[Seijin Shiki]].
Suwa shrine is also the host to many annual festivals. These festivals serve the joint purpose of honoring the kami, providing a sense of community for shrine worshipers, and providing exposure and income for the shrine itself. Some of these festivals are typical to all major Shinto shrines in Japan, but some are unique to Suwa shrine.
* '''Atomic bomb commemorative service''' - This festival is probably unique in all of Japan, in that it combines elements of Christian, Buddhist and Shinto worship to pray for the more than 70,000 people killed by the atomic bomb dropped on Nagasaki. It is an annual festival, taking place every year on August 9th.
* '''Doll's Festival''' - Like all of Japan, Suwa Shrine observes the [[Hina Matsuri|Doll's Festival]] on March 3rd. However, since the 1980s the festival has been observed in an unusual way, in that attractive, 20-year old women are selected to wear the shrine's collection of antique [[kimono]] and pose in a live Doll's Day procession. The competition amongst the young women is fierce, and there are far more applicants than positions in the festival. The two most beautiful women are chosen for the key roles of the Emperor and Emperess in the procession. This tradition was created largely as a [[media event]] to promote and advertise Suwa shrine, and every year television crews are invited to broadcast the festival.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
* '''[[Kunchi|Okunchi]]''' - The most famous of Suwa Shrine's festivals, this festival begain along with the founding of the shrine as a way to both demonstrate its new importance in the community and as a way to hunt for [[Kakure Kirishitan|Hidden Christians]]. Kunchi festival is held from [[October]] 7 to 9 every year and is considered one of the major festivals of [[Japan]], along with the [[Gion Matsuri]] and Osaka's Tenjin Matsuri. It has been designated as an Important Intangible Cultural Asset.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
* '''Yutate-sai''' - A theatrical ritual, originally performed by [[Yamabushi]], a priest demonstrates his communion with the kami by plunging his hands into boiling water unharmed. This ritual was performed on the opening of Suwa shrine, and is continued to be performed to this day. It is very rare at other Shinto shrines
==English language fortunes==
Most Shinto shrines sell Omikuji, a type of fortune telling that comes on a slip of paper that lists your particular luck at that time. Suwa shrine was the first shrine in Japan to offer Omikuchi in English.
The fortunes are divided into:
* Best luck * Average luck * Good luck * Half luck * Partly bad luck * Worst luck * Bad luck but turns good * Luck in the end
Visitors tie bands of paper round the legs of the stone-carved [[Imperial guardian lions|guardian lions]] and pray for help with things such as [[Smoking cessation|stopping smoking]] and [[Entrance examination|success in examinations.]]

It has been designated as an Important Intangible Cultural Asset.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
* '''Yutate-sai''' - A theatrical ritual, originally performed by [[Yamabushi]], a priest demonstrates his communion with the kami by plunging his hands into boiling water unharmed. This ritual was performed on the opening of Suwa shrine, and is continued to be performed to this day. It is very rare at other Shinto shrines
==English language fortunes==
Most Shinto shrines sell Omikuji, a type of fortune telling that comes on a slip of paper that lists your particular luck at that time. Suwa shrine was the first shrine in Japan to offer Omikuchi in English.
The fortunes are divided into:
* Best luck * Average luck * Good luck * Half luck * Partly bad luck * Worst luck * Bad luck but turns good * Luck in the end
Visitors tie bands of paper round the legs of the stone-carved [[Imperial guardian lions|guardian lions]] and pray for help with things such as [[Smoking cessation|stopping smoking]] and [[Entrance examination|success in examinations.]]
==References== <div class="references"><references/>
==Further Reading==
*Nelson, John K. [http://www.uwosh.edu/faculty_staff/earns/suwa.html Historical monuments at Nagasaki Suwa shrine]
{{coor title dms|32|45|16|N|129|52|54|E|region:JP-42_type:landmark_source:dewiki}}
[[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki]]
[[de:Suwa-Schrein (Nagasaki)]] [[ja:鎮西大社諏訪神社]]

'''Suwa Shrine''' (諏訪神社) is the major [[Shinto shrine]] of [[Nagasaki]], [[Japan]], and home to the [[Kunchi|Kunchi festival]]. It is located in the northern part of the city, on the slopes of Mount Konpira. The shrine in Nagasaki is one of many [[Suwa Shrine|Suwa shrines]], all of which are dedicated to Suwa-no-Kami, a [[kami]] of valor and duty, and are linked with [[Suwa Taisha]], the head shrine of Suwa-no-Kami worship.
While most Shinto shrines have a traditional and spiritual story of foundation, Suwa shrine is fairly unique in that it was established for political reasons, as a way of stopping and reverting the conversion to Christianity that had swept over much of Nagasaki. In modern times it remains an important and succesful center of the community.
==History==
The official date of construction for Suwa Shrine is [[1614]], the same year as [[Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokygawa Ieyasu's]] Edict against Christianity, although there was little more than a small structure to mark the position of the future shrine at this time. At that time, Nagasaki was home to the largest Christian population in Japan, and had destroyed many of the former Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples. The [[Tokugawa Shogunate|Tokugawa]] [[Bakufu]] government had taken power and reversed its [[Kirishitan#Japan.27s_policy_toward_Catholicism|friendly policy towards Christianity]]. The government had begun forcing [[Christians]] to reconvert to the Japanese religions of [[Shinbutsu shūgō|Buddhism and Shinto]]. It was thought that establishing a major point of Shinto worship would be important to these efforts, giving the local population a central point of worship and a sense of community.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
The temporary stucture was frequently attacted by resisting Christians, until [[1624]] when Aoki Kensei came to Nagasaki. His religious zeal and skill at organizing, combined with authority granted by the leading Yoshida Shinto council, lead to the completion of the main structure of Suwa Shrine.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref> In order to attract attention and encourage attendence at the new shrine, a dramatic ''yutate-sai'' ritual, where a priest demonstrates his communion with the kami by plunging his hands into boiling water unharmed, was performed. In spite of this, as well as the [[Sumo|sumo match]] that followed, very few people came to watch.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
In [[1634]], an edict was issued requiring all local people to register at the shrine and be counted. In addition to this, in another attempt to further increase local participation in shrine events, a great [[Japanese festivals|autumn festival]] was held at the [[shrine]]. Both of these events were created in the attempt to discover any remaining Christians, who would not be able paricipate in the festival or register at the shrine. Anyone who failed to do so was arrested, tortured and possibly executed if they did not renounce their Christian faith.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
Because Nagasaki was the only place in Japan with an [[Dejima|open port]], it was considered essential to impress the Dutch and Chinese traders with Japanese culture. In addition to the grand festival, from [[1638]] major performances of [[Noh]] were also held at Suwa shrine, at the direct command of the Shogun. These performances continued until [[1856]], when a major fire at the shrine destroyed most of the expensive Noh masks and props.
Suwa Shrine survived the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki|atomic bombing of Nagasaki]] on [[August 9]], [[1945]]. It is thought to have survived intact to its strategic location in the central part of Mount Konpira's southern flank, although in the aftermath of the bomb local residents were quick to note that while the famous [[Urakami Cathedral]] and surrounding Catholic neighborhoods were obliterated, the Shinto shine still stood.
This was considered to be signifigant by the survivers of the bombing, showing the power of the native Japanese kami as opposed to the imported Christian god. In addition, priests from Suwa shrine took an active role in the rebuilding of Nagasaki, including consecrating the land and purifying structures that still stood.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
==Festivals==
Like most [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]], every year thousands of people visit Suwa [[Shinto]] shrine to pray for peace and prosperity. The Shrine is also the main destination in Nagasaki for festivals such as [[Shichi-Go-San]] and [[Seijin Shiki]].
Suwa shrine is also the host to many annual festivals. These festivals serve the joint purpose of honoring the kami, providing a sense of community for shrine worshipers, and providing exposure and income for the shrine itself. Some of these festivals are typical to all major Shinto shrines in Japan, but some are unique to Suwa shrine.
* '''Atomic bomb commemorative service''' - This festival is probably unique in all of Japan, in that it combines elements of Christian, Buddhist and Shinto worship to pray for the more than 70,000 people killed by the atomic bomb dropped on Nagasaki. It is an annual festival, taking place every year on August 9th.
* '''Doll's Festival''' - Like all of Japan, Suwa Shrine observes the [[Hina Matsuri|Doll's Festival]] on March 3rd. However, since the 1980s the festival has been observed in an unusual way, in that attractive, 20-year old women are selected to wear the shrine's collection of antique [[kimono]] and pose in a live Doll's Day procession. The competition amongst the young women is fierce, and there are far more applicants than positions in the festival. The two most beautiful women are chosen for the key roles of the Emperor and Emperess in the procession. This tradition was created largely as a [[media event]] to promote and advertise Suwa shrine, and every year television crews are invited to broadcast the festival.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
* '''[[Kunchi|Okunchi]]''' - The most famous of Suwa Shrine's festivals, this festival begain along with the founding of the shrine as a way to both demonstrate its new importance in the community and as a way to hunt for [[Kakure Kirishitan|Hidden Christians]]. Kunchi festival is held from [[October]] 7 to 9 every year and is considered one of the major festivals of [[Japan]], along with the [[Gion Matsuri]] and Osaka's Tenjin Matsuri. It has been designated as an Important Intangible Cultural Asset.<ref name="Nelson">Nelson, John K., ''A Year in the Life of a Shinto Shrine, USA, University of Washington Press, 1996, ISBN 0-295-97500-8</ref>
* '''Yutate-sai''' - A theatrical ritual, originally performed by [[Yamabushi]], a priest demonstrates his communion with the kami by plunging his hands into boiling water unharmed. This ritual was performed on the opening of Suwa shrine, and is continued to be performed to this day. It is very rare at other Shinto shrines
==English language fortunes==
Most Shinto shrines sell Omikuji, a type of fortune telling that comes on a slip of paper that lists your particular luck at that time. Suwa shrine was the first shrine in Japan to offer Omikuchi in English.
The fortunes are divided into:
* Best luck * Average luck * Good luck * Half luck * Partly bad luck * Worst luck * Bad luck but turns good * Luck in the end
Visitors tie bands of paper round the legs of the stone-carved [[Imperial guardian lions|guardian lions]] and pray for help with things such as [[Smoking cessation|stopping smoking]] and [[Entrance examination|success in examinations.]]
==References== <div class="references"><references/>
==Further Reading==
*Nelson, John K. [http://www.uwosh.edu/faculty_staff/earns/suwa.html Historical monuments at Nagasaki Suwa shrine]
